title: There's Only One Google Smart Speaker Worth Buying Now
gist: Wired's buying guide covers Google's consolidation of its smart speaker lineup down to a single $100 Home Speaker and the rollout of Gemini for Home (replacing Google Assistant free across most existing devices), with advanced features gated behind a $10-$20/month Google Home Premium subscription that bundles the AI assistant with Nest camera video storage, positioned against Amazon's paid Alexa+.

insights.claim: Google has consolidated its smart speaker hardware lineup to a single $100 product, the Home Speaker, which replaces both the Nest Audio and Nest Mini, the narrowest hardware lineup in Google's smart speaker history.
insights.speaker: Nena Farrell
insights.so_what: Signals Google deprioritizing smart-speaker hardware diversity in favor of a single SKU, likely concentrating investment in the Gemini software/subscription layer rather than device variety.
insights.quote: this single speaker has replaced both the Nest Audio and Nest Mini
insights.locator: Google's always had a limited range of speakers compared to Amazon

insights.claim: Google's flagship smart display, the Nest Hub Max, dates to 2019 and remains the company's top recommended display in 2026, with no indication a Gemini-native display refresh is imminent, showing Google's hardware refresh cadence for displays has stalled even as its AI assistant layer is actively updated.
insights.speaker: Nena Farrell
insights.so_what: A signal Google is decoupling AI/software investment (fast iteration) from hardware investment (slow/stalled), useful for gauging Google's capital allocation priorities in smart home versus rivals still shipping new hardware.
insights.quote: I'm hoping we'll see a new Google smart display designed for Gemini soon, but I also wouldn't be surprised if we don't. Google really doesn't update its lineup often
insights.locator: The Older Smart Display That's Still Great

insights.claim: The Google Pixel Tablet, despite being one of Google's most recent devices, is explicitly excluded from Gemini for Home when docked in its speaker base, and the product 'has been disappearing from shelves,' implying Google is quietly discontinuing it as a smart-home product.
insights.speaker: Nena Farrell
insights.so_what: An early signal of product-line pruning at Google; investors tracking Google hardware SKUs should expect the Pixel Tablet/Speaker Base combo to be phased out.
insights.quote: This product has been disappearing from shelves, so it's likely Google won't continue selling it, at least not as a smart home device.
insights.locator: The most notable exclusion is one of Google's most recent devices

insights.claim: Third-party smart displays that previously ran Google Assistant, such as the Lenovo Smart Display, will not receive Gemini support and remain frozen on the legacy assistant, showing Google's AI upgrade path is now gated to its own first-party hardware.
insights.speaker: Nena Farrell
insights.so_what: Confirms Google is consolidating its AI assistant ecosystem around first-party hardware, closing off the earlier open third-party smart-display strategy, relevant to how defensible Google's smart-home hardware business is becoming.
insights.quote: Google no longer updates software for these third-party displays, and I don't expect them to get Gemini access.
insights.locator: What Happened to Third-Party Smart Displays That Worked With Google?

themes.theme: Gemini for Home Monetization vs. Amazon

insights.claim: Google replaced Google Assistant with a new AI assistant, Gemini for Home, rolled out free to nearly all existing Google smart speakers and displays, positioning it against Amazon's paid Alexa+ assistant.
insights.speaker: Nena Farrell
insights.so_what: Google is using free baseline AI assistant access as a competitive wedge against Amazon's subscription-gated Alexa+, a notable divergence in smart-home AI monetization strategy between the two ecosystem owners.
insights.quote: you can use it for free, unlike Amazon's similar new assistant, Alexa+

insights.claim: Google monetizes advanced Gemini for Home features via a 'Google Home Premium' subscription that bundles the AI assistant's Gemini Live conversational mode and AI routine-builder with Nest camera and doorbell video storage, priced at $10/month (standard) or $20/month (advanced) rather than charging for the base assistant itself.
insights.speaker: Nena Farrell
insights.so_what: Reveals Google's monetization wedge is video storage and smart-home services bundled with AI, not the assistant itself, a business-model data point for valuing Google's smart-home subscription revenue versus Amazon's Alexa+ model.
insights.quote: The new Google Home Premium plan bundles the subscriptions for smart home assistants with storage plans for smart home video products

insights.claim: In the reviewer's informal head-to-head testing, Gemini for Home answered questions slightly better than Amazon's Alexa+.
insights.speaker: Nena Farrell
insights.so_what: An early qualitative signal that Google may be closing or reversing the historical assistant-quality gap with Amazon in the voice-assistant race, worth validating against broader benchmarks.
insights.quote: Gemini did slightly better at answering my questions than Alexa+.
insights.locator: Overall, it's a great speaker and my new favorite overall smart speaker
